data.title: What to know about 'UFC 250' at the White House
data.description:
The Ultimate Fighting Championship (UFC) is bringing its high-octane show to Washington, D.C., next month, staging a prime-time fight at the White House as part of the country’s “America 250” celebrations and commemorating President Trump’s 80th birthday.

Preparations have been underway for weeks ahead of the widely buzzed-about combat sports spectacle, which the president is expected to attend. It will combine politics and sports in a way rarely seen, beginning with the massive set constructed on the South Lawn. 

Here’s what to know about “UFC 250” on June 14:

Trump’s ties to UFC run deep

The president has long been a fan of combat sports and was known for hosting top boxing matches and prize fights at his casinos and resorts in the years before he ran for office.  

Trump has appeared at several top UFC fights during both his terms and forged a close friendship with UFC chief executive Dana White in the process.

Steven Cheung, one of the president’s closest communications advisers, has worked for UFC and the president’s daughter Ivanka was instrumental in securing the “UFC 250” event.

The June 14 fight was initially Trump’s idea, White has said in a number of interviews. The administration is hoping the spectacle will inspire patriotism among Americans at a time many are feeling uncertain about the economy, foreign conflicts overseas and other issues.

Yet White, who stumped for Trump at the 2024 Republican National Convention, insists “UFC 250” will not be an inherently political event.

“You can make anything political if you want to,” he said as part of a wide-ranging interview with Time published this week. “I love this country like anybody on the left loves this country. I love this country like anybody on the right loves this country.”

White says he is confident the fight night will “positively deliver” for both the president and his organization.   

Trump, who has experience promoting large-scale events, oftentimes choreographed specifically for television, has also worked overtime to hype the event.

“I’ve been involved in a lot of big events; I have never had an event that has had more interest than the UFC fight we have right at the front door,” he told reporters earlier this year.  

Top UFC fighters set to participate

UFC in March unveiled its full fight card for matches set to play out in the octagon erected on the South Lawn.  

The fights themselves, which begin in late afternoon and are expected to run deep into the evening, will feature top names from across the sport and feature six prime-time bouts.

The main event will pit lightweight Georgian fighter Ilia Topuria against American Justin Gaethje, while undercard clashes between Brazilian Alex Pereira and France’s Ciryl Gane are also scheduled.

American Sean O’Malley will take on Canada’s Aiemann Zahabi, while American Michael Chandler is slated to take on Brazilian fighter Mauricio Ruffy.

UFC will host two fight nights leading into the June 14 event, on Saturday in Macau and June 6 in Las Vegas, and the league has been promoting the White House showdowns aggressively since plans were announced.  

There are also expected to be a number of celebrities in attendance, with a guest list that reportedly includes Hollywood icons like Dwayne “The Rock” Johnson, Adam Sandler and Mario Lopez.

“We’ve got to get it all dialed in, and then we have to execute that night,” White told Time. “We’re putting on the most historic sporting event in history, and now we have to deliver.”

White House grounds transformed into outdoor arena

Hype for the first-of-its-kind event was kicked into overdrive over the weekend when photos emerged of construction crews beginning work on the South Lawn.

The photos showed workers building pieces of the red and blue UFC fighting ring with the “UFC Freedom 250” signage and massive cranes constructing risers and other large buildouts to accommodate guests.

The setup will include a regulation UFC Octagon, which spans 750 square feet and will serve as a canvas for tilts between fighters who will reportedly walk out of the Oval Office before doing battle.

The president has said the goal is for the space to hold 4,500 fans, and he anticipates another 50,000 to 100,000 people will be able to watch the bouts on “massive screens” staged at the Ellipse, a public park located directly south of the White House.

Some on social media have mocked the construction and pointed it out as an eyesore, which comes as the administration continues work on the East Wing, where the president is planning a controversial ballroom.

Some UFC figures aren’t impressed

Some in the Mixed Martial Arts and professional fighting world are less than enthused about the prospect of the event.

“I don’t like the idea of fighting outside at all. There are too many problems with it,” said Joe Rogan, a popular podcaster on the right and onetime ally of the president who also serves as a UFC announcer. “In June, in DC, we looked it up last year — the same day was 100 degrees.”

The White House did not respond to a request for comment seeking information on a weather contingency plan.

Rogan called it a mistake, from UFC’s perspective, to host a championship fight in a “non-controlled environment.”

“It should be inside an air-conditioned arena,” said Rogan, who has also called the spectacle a “gimmick” and a “security nightmare.”

The president has brushed aside such a suggestion.

“At first, I thought, ‘That’s not nice,’” the president told Time. “And then I realized, it is a gimmick. Life is a gimmick, if you think about it, right? But it’s a good gimmick. It’s something that will never happen again.”

Both Trump and UFC stand to gain a great deal with their core base of supporters with “UFC 250.”

White suggested during a recent interview with NPR the average UFC fan and a supporter of the president have much in common and the June fight will be major moment for a key demographic he is targeting with his business.

“During the COVID craziness and a lot of the stuff that went on, a lot of young men felt displaced and a lot of negative things were being said about young men,” White said. “This sport speaks to young men … and you know, we supported Trump.”

data.title: Red, white and Trump: How America's 250th is drawing controversy
data.description:
​President Trump is eagerly celebrating America’s 250th birthday in a big way, taking extraordinary steps to incorporate his brand and likeness into the revelry celebrations and courting controversy along the way.

Trump’s actions, which include hosting a UFC fight on the White House lawn and efforts to put his face on a $250 bill, have raised accusations that he is politicizing events meant to celebrate the nation’s birth.

Just this past week at a White House Cabinet meeting, Trump promoted bright red $55 hats sold by his family company in honor of the America 250 celebrations. And in a span of two days, performers were announced for a two-week-long National Mall event, and many quickly withdrew their participation, claiming they were misled about the political nature of it.  

Presidential historian Craig Shirley argued that other presidents have put their spin on America’s birthday, and now Trump’s taking his own “traditional and unique” approach.

“Trump won the election last November [2024], so he gets to enjoy the spoils and the perks of power,” Shirley said. “That’s just the way the system works.”

Although he joked about Trump’s planned UFC fight: “I doubt the founders thought of that.”

Leonard Steinhorn, professor of communication and affiliate professor of history at American University, contrasted Trump’s actions with those of former President Gerald Ford, who used the country’s 200th birthday celebration as an “opportunity to celebrate America and its heritage and its tradition,” while Trump “has made everything about him.”

“In 1976 it was about us and 2026 it’s about Donald Trump, and I think that’s a fundamental difference, and it may be why you’re going to have a very divided electorate on this celebration,” Steinhorn said.

In 1926, former President Calvin Coolidge traveled to Philadelphia to give a speech and attend the Sesquicentennial Exposition to celebrate America’s 150th. His face appeared on the 1926 Sesquicentennial of American Independence Half Dollar, making him the only living president to ever appear on a U.S. coin.

Ford, for the country’s bicentennial in 1976, went on a more than 500-mile journey on July 4 to attend events in Washington, D.C., Pennsylvania and New York City, according to the White House Historical Association.

He was fighting for the Republican Party’s nomination against Ronald Reagan at the time and “just draped himself in the bicentennial,” Shirley said, and “politicized” some of the festivities by bringing uncommitted Republican delegates to watch the fireworks in New York.

But others argue Trump has gone too far.

“Donald Trump and his henchmen have sabotaged what should be a unifying moment and appear intent on instead creating a highly divisive, corporate-funded, ideologically extremist exercise,” Lisa Gilbert, co-president of Public Citizen, said in a statement.

Barbara Perry, co-chair of the University of Virginia’s Miller Center Presidential Oral History Program, said Trump is treating the celebrations as a businessman would.

“Branding and business have been his business, and the fact that he puts his name on everything before he became president, I guess, shouldn’t surprise us that he wants his presidency and legacy to be known by his name and his name only,” Perry said.

“So this is just another opportunity, along with the Olympics that will be in LA, and the soccer tournaments that will be going on, the World Cup coming to the U.S., these are all opportunities for him to continue to brand himself and to create his legacy,” she said.

Perry, who was an intern on Capitol Hill in 1976, also pointed to Ford as a contrast to how Trump has handled the celebrations.

“So here it is, July 4, 1976, a few months before the general presidential election, which would turn out to be very close, he lost barely to Jimmy Carter, and that would have been the time for a president to put his name on everything and brand everything about this patriotic celebration about himself,” she said. “He did not do that.”

Administration officials are pushing to put the president’s face on a $250 bill, along with a commemorative 24-karat gold coin, limited-edition passports, national park passes and other memorabilia. The Trump Organization has also filed several trademark applications for Trump logos, like “Trump 250.”

Federal law states that no living person should appear on U.S. currency. Administration officials have pointed to legislation pending in Congress that could change that.

But Trump has revealed even grander plans for the nation’s capital to celebrate the 250th — the UFC match that falls on his 80th birthday next month, an Indy Car race through the streets of the capital in August and even construction of a 250-foot triumphal arch.

The arch project has been approved by the U.S. Commission of Fine Arts, which is filled with Trump appointees. But the administration has faced pushback for the planned triumphal arch near the Lincoln Memorial. A group of veterans has sued to stop the construction of it, and House Democrats have introduced a bill to block it, calling it a “vanity project.” 

When asked if the planned $250 bill was appropriate as Americans struggle to afford gas and groceries, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent told reporters Thursday that “it’s bifurcated.”

He said the 250th celebrations were happening “because it’s being funded by private citizens, by the federal government, by state governments, by municipal governments to celebrate our country, and I don’t think that there’s anything untoward about having the president of the United States on the 250th anniversary bill.”

After announcing the first wave of performers for the upcoming “Great American State Fair” on the mall, more than half of the nine artists pulled out, saying it was a political event that they thought was nonpolitical.

“I was presented with an opportunity to perform at a nonpartisan event, but that turned out to be misleading,” Martina McBride, one of the announced performers, said on social media as she pulled out of the event.

The event was organized by Freedom 250, the public-private partnership that Trump launched last year. He appointed Keith Krach, his former under secretary of State, as the CEO.

It describes itself as a “national, non-partisan organization leading the celebration of our Nation’s 250th birthday” and is separate from America250, the bipartisan initiative created by Congress in 2016.

Julia Friedland, spokesperson for Freedom 250, told “Sunrise on The Hill” Friday that the event is “inherently nonpolitical.”

“It is a celebration of our country,” she said.

A New York Times report from February said perks are being given to those who donate at least $1 million to Freedom 250, like invites to a special reception or a speaking role if they shell out $2.5 million or more. VIP access and preferred seating would be given to those who donate at least $500,000.

When Trump announced the creation of Freedom 250 in a video posted on social media late last year, he said he promised to “give America the most spectacular birthday party the world has ever seen.”

White House spokesperson Davis Ingle said, “President Trump is working 24/7 to Make America Great Again, including his historic beautification of our Nation’s Capital and giving it the glory it deserves during our historic semiquincentennial celebration – something everyone should celebrate. 

“Only people who suffer from a severe and debilitating disease known as Trump Derangement Syndrome would find a problem with that,” he said.

data.title: Trump focuses on keeping Ebola out of US over fighting outbreak abroad
data.description:
The Trump administration has turned inward in response to the Ebola outbreak in Congo and Uganda, with increasingly isolationist policies that are alarming and disappointing infectious disease experts.  

According to the World Health Organization (WHO), suspected Ebola cases have passed 1,000, with nearly 250 suspected deaths. The outbreak is growing rapidly, and countries around the world have mobilized with funds, personnel and medical assistance. 

The U.S. is providing more than $100 million in financial assistance for affected countries, including $80 million to scale up the procurement and delivery of personal protective equipment. 

The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) and the State Department say they are ensuring rapid viral testing is available and are actively deploying State Department resources in Congo and Uganda. 

“The most effective way to protect Americans is to stop outbreaks as quickly as possible, where they occur. That’s why CDC response focuses both on supporting affected countries and maintaining readiness here at home,” Satish Pillai, the CDC’s Ebola response leader, said during a briefing with reporters Friday. 

Yet to veterans of past Ebola outbreaks and other infectious disease experts, the Trump administration appears to be more focused on keeping Ebola out of the U.S. than helping fight the outbreak abroad. 

“The United States’ highest priority remains protecting the health and security of the American people by working to prevent the Ebola outbreak from reaching our shores,” a U.S. Department of State spokesperson said Thursday. 

The spokesperson echoed comments made by Secretary of State Marco Rubio during a Cabinet meeting Wednesday, when he said the administration “will not allow any cases of Ebola to enter the United States.” 

Shortly after the U.S. first learned of the outbreak, the administration temporarily banned entry from noncitizen travelers who had been in Congo, Uganda or South Sudan over the last 21-day period.  

The entry ban was later extended to green-card holders.  

“Travel restrictions may delay the introduction of a pathogen, but history has shown they rarely prevent it entirely,” said Krutika Kuppalli, an infectious diseases specialist in Texas who ran an Ebola treatment center in Sierra Leone during the 2014-2016 West Africa outbreak. 

Now, the U.S. is planning to require any American who may have been exposed to Ebola to quarantine for 21 days in Kenya, rather than return to the U.S.  

Those plans were temporarily interrupted Friday when a Kenyan high court blocked further construction and barred medical operations at the facility while a lawsuit objecting to the plan is pending.  

Still, the fact that high ranking administration officials are going to such lengths to keep anyone even potentially exposed to the virus away from the U.S. could be disheartening and a deterrence to the people with the right skills who could provide the most help on the ground. 

“If individuals believe they may not be repatriated, should they become ill or exposed, it could affect willingness to participate in future response efforts,” Kuppalli said.  

Officials have said if Americans contract Ebola and need advanced medical care, they will be sent to Europe, even though the U.S. is home to a specialized network of containment beds in facilities funded with billions of federal dollars.  

During the 2014-2016 Ebola epidemic, multiple Americans were safely repatriated to the United States for monitoring and treatment in those centers, and there were no secondary cases associated with those medical transports. 

“There’s nothing like this in the entire world, right? There are isolated places that can do this sort of thing, but a big network of preparedness centers with capacity for intensive care, and very importantly, staff who are [trained]” just doesn’t exist, said Jeanne Marrazzo, chief executive officer of the Infectious Diseases Society of America. 

The White House appears “to have completely shredded the playbook that we have used for this sort of thing, I think with very good success in the past,” Marrazzo said. “Nobody is really detailing a comprehensive plan.” 

Seven Americans who had been exposed to Ebola in Congo were flown to Europe, including a doctor with a confirmed infection.  

Another doctor who was exposed to the virus was sent to Prague in the Czech Republic. 

The virus — a rare strain called Bundibugyo, against which there are no proven vaccines or treatments — spread undetected for weeks. The virus is fatal in about 50 percent of cases, and the World Health Organization declared the outbreak a public health emergency of international concern only two days after it was recognized. 

But the Trump administration is no longer a part of the WHO and has dismantled the U.S. Agency for International Development. Locking down the border to foreign nationals and even American citizens sends a message that the U.S. isn’t interested in being an international health leader anymore, Kuppalli said.  

“For decades, the United States has been viewed as a leader in outbreak preparedness and response. Policies that emphasize distancing the U.S. from affected regions rather than supporting response efforts on the ground may raise questions among partners about future U.S. engagement during international health emergencies,” Kuppalli said.

data.title: White House memo says Trump 'remains in excellent health' after physical
data.description:
President Trump “remains in excellent health,” his physician wrote in a memo released by the White House late Friday following the president’s annual physical exam earlier this week.

Trump visited Walter Reed National Military Medical Center on Tuesday for the physical, where he underwent cardiac, neurological, dermatologic and other types of examinations, according to White House physician Capt. Sean Barbabella.

“President Trump remains in excellent health, demonstrating strong cardiac, pulmonary, neurological, and overall physical function,” Barbabella wrote in a summary at the end of the report. “His demanding daily schedule, including multiple high-level meetings, public engagements, and regular physical activity, continues to support his overall well-being.

“Cognitive and physical performance are excellent,” the physician concluded. “He is fully fit to carry out all duties of the Commander-in-Chief and Head of State.”

Trump had already said he “checked out perfectly” in a post on Truth Social after the visit to Walter Reed. It was his third visit to the medical center in 13 months. The president’s off-the-cuff disclosure that he underwent an MRI scan last fall raised some questions about his health.

The president weighed 238 pounds during Tuesday’s physical exam, according to the White House memo — an increase from Trump’s weight of 224 pounds noted in the results of his April 2025 annual visit.

Barbabella noted scarring on Trump’s right ear consistent with the injury he suffered during an assassination attempt in July 2024. The memo also noted slight lower leg swelling.

The physicial wrote of bruising on Trump’s hands “consistent with minor soft tissue irritation related to frequent handshaking in the setting of aspirin use for cardiovascular prevention,” which the president has addressed before.

Trump also scored a 30 out of 30 in the Montreal Cognitive Assessment, a test commonly used to detect memory issues or cognitive impairment.

Trump is set to turn 80 on June 14. He was the oldest president to take the oath of office when he was inaugurated for a second time last January.

data.title: Bovino cheers ICE agents at New Jersey facility criticized for violence: 'Give them hell'
data.description:
Former U.S. Border Patrol commander-at-large Greg Bovino issued a message of support to immigration agents on Friday amid clashes between federal officers and protesters outside of Delaney Hall immigration detention center in New Jersey this week. 

Bovino urged Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agents to “give them hell and live the moment!!” in his post. 

“ICE Agents at Delaney, hang in there,” Bovino wrote. 

“You have the world watching and supporting your efforts to hold the line,” he continued. “Every one of us wants to be shoulder to shoulder with you. In speaking with the Mean Green Team, they send you support and are wishing you the best.”

Bovino retired from the agency earlier this year after being demoted following the fatal shootings of two people by federal agents during the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown in Minnesota. 

Protests outside Delaney Hall escalated after New Jersey Gov. Mikie Sherrill (D) was denied access to the facility. The demonstrators are demanding that the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) provide answers for the alleged mistreatment of detainees. 

Sherrill said in a video shared on social media on Friday that she is “continuing to take every action” to get a health department inspection of this private facility and to close it entirely.

“I’ll also keep calling on everyone there now, especially ICE, to de-escalate the situation, to act humanely, to protest peacefully and to respect the laws and the constitution of the United States,” she said.

Democratic Sen. Andy Kim (N.J.) told CNN that he experienced eye irritation after DHS agents launched pepper balls at a crowd of protesters on Monday outside the New Jersey detention facility. 

“I tried my best to get in between the ICE agents and the crowd as the ICE convoy decided to just go ahead and plow through the crowd, which was absolutely just so dangerous of an action that they were doing,” Kim said. 

The lawmaker, who sits on the Senate Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs, told the outlet that he was not “hit directly” by the pepper balls. DHS said none of the protesters were “directly struck” by these projectiles.  

“But more broadly, this is something that was avoidable,” he said. “We were trying to find a way to be able to move forward that wasn’t going to escalate in this way, but ICE decided that they just had enough and they were just going to move ahead, and I just thought that was an incredibly dangerous action for them to do and that continued problems, that continued threat of violence and escalation continues today, tomorrow. 

“I mean, we’re seeing just a heightened level of danger right now in New Jersey,” he continued.

DHS Secretary Markwayne Mullin said in a social media post on Friday evening that immigration officers sustained injuries after a protester bit them on Thursday. 

“Last night, a violent rioter savagely kicked and bit ICE law enforcement officers outside of Delaney Hall. Today, this violent agitator is being charged,” Mullin wrote in the post.

“The Trump Administration will ALWAYS stand with our federal law enforcement officers,” he continued. “Anyone who assaults a law enforcement officer will be prosecuted to the fullest extent of the law.”

data.title: US military kills 3 in latest strike on suspected drug boat in eastern Pacific
data.description:
The U.S. military on Friday killed three men in its latest strike on a boat accused of carrying drugs in the eastern Pacific Ocean.

U.S. Southern Command (Southcom) wrote in a post on the social platform X that the vessel was “transiting along known narco-trafficking routes” and being operated by designated terrorist organizations, but the post did not name the terror groups. Southcom Commander Gen. Francis L. Donovan ordered the strike.

No U.S. military forces were harmed and no survivors from the alleged drug boat were noted.

“SOUTHCOM is unwavering in its commitment to applying total systemic friction on the cartels,” reads the post, which is accompanied by a video showing a boat exploding and then engulfed in flames and smoke.

Since September, the Trump administration has targeted roughly 60 boats in the Caribbean Sea and eastern Pacific, resulting in about 200 total deaths under a campaign called Operation Southern Spear. The U.S. military had already killed a total of three people in two separate strikes earlier this week, with the Tuesday attack leaving two survivors.

Democratic and some Republican lawmakers, retired military officials and war experts have repeatedly questioned the legality of the strikes. The Defense Department’s inspector general recently began investigating the framework being used to launch the attacks, according to a May 11 letter sent to Under Secretary of Defense for Intelligence and Security Bradley D. Hansell.

Earlier Friday, Southcom announced that Donovan met with Cuban military officials for a “brief exchange on operational security matters” near Naval Station Guantánamo Bay.

data.title: Southcom commander meets with Cuban military officials as Trump amps up pressure on island
data.description:
The head of U.S. Southern Command (Southcom) met with senior leaders of the Cuban military on Friday amid heightened tensions between the two countries’ governments. 

Commander Gen. Francis L. Donovan joined Cuba’s First Deputy Minister of the Chief of the General Staff Roberto Legrá Sotolongo and other leaders near Naval Station Guantánamo Bay, Southcom announced in a statement shared on social media. 

The Cuban and American military leaders engaged in a “brief exchange on operational security measures,” according to Southcom’s Friday statement. 

The U.S. military commander also led a perimeter security assessment and discussed “operational readiness” with officials at the base during his Friday visit. 

“Naval Station Guantanamo Bay is a vital operational and logistical hub that supports U.S. military efforts to counter threats that undermine security, stability and democracy in our hemisphere,” Southcom wrote in its Friday statement announcing Donovan’s visit. 

The Trump administration has ramped up its opposition to the island’s communist government in recent weeks. The president has suggested he could launch a military invasion on the country. 

Secretary of State Marco Rubio, a Cuban American, has repeatedly condemned the island’s leadership. However, he acknowledged last week that the “likelihood” of a negotiated agreement with Cuban leadership is “not high.”

GOP Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.) suggested last week that leadership change in the country could be “close at hand.”

“I believe the liberation of the wonderful people of Cuba from the clutches of communism is close at hand,” Graham wrote in a post on social platform X.

This pressure comes as the Justice Department moved to indict former Cuban President Raúl Castro on murder charges on the country’s independence day last Wednesday. 

Trump told reporters following Castro’s indictment that his administration has Cuba “on our mind.” The USS Nimitz aircraft carrier and its ships are stationed in the Caribbean, Southcom announced last week.

data.title: Trump snubs Mace with S.C. gov endorsement of Pam Evette
data.description:
President Trump endorsed a GOP challenger to Rep. Nancy Mace’s (R-S.C.) campaign for South Carolina governor on Friday, backing Lt. Gov. Pam Evette in the contest. 

The president lauded Evette as an “America First Patriot” who “never wavered” in a Truth Social post on Friday evening. 

“Pam Evette is a good friend, fighter, and WINNER, and will be a terrific Governor of South Carolina,” Trump wrote. “Pam has my Complete and Total Endorsement — SHE WILL NEVER LET YOU DOWN!”

Mace responded in a social media post on Friday evening, suggesting that her advocacy for the release of the Justice Department’s files related to its probe into convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein contributed to the president’s decision not to back her. 

“I know I put the likelihood of an endorsement on the line when I demanded transparency on the Epstein files,” the lawmaker wrote. “I demanded it because you deserved the truth – ALL OF IT – and as a survivor of a corrupt and broken court system, I will always pursue justice for those who deserve it.”

“If sacrificing my values is the price of an endorsement, I will never pay it,” she continued. 

Mace was part of a bipartisan effort to pass the Epstein Files Transparency Act, which the president signed into law last year. 

Trump’s Friday endorsement also snubbed Rep. Ralph Norman (R), who had taken a slight lead on Mace in recent polling. 

A Trafalgar Group survey released this week found Evette and state Attorney General Alan Wilson in a dead heat among Republican primary voters, with Norman and Mace trailing behind. The state’s primary will be held on June 9. 

The president made several other endorsements on Friday, including a surprise decision to back “MAGA warrior” Mike Mazzei in the Oklahoma governor’s race. 

Mazzei, the former chair of the Oklahoma Senate Finance Committee and former state budget secretary, is polling behind two other GOP candidates in the race. 

“As your next Governor, Mike will fight tirelessly to Support our Amazing Farmers and Ranchers, Keep our Border SECURE, Stop Migrant Crime, Ensure LAW AND ORDER, Strengthen our Military/Veterans, and Defend our always under siege Second Amendment,” Trump wrote in a Truth Social post. 

The GOP candidate said he was “honored” to receive the endorsement in a social media post and thanked the president for his “friendship and support.”

The Oklahoma primary election will take place on June 16. 

Trump has championed his recent endorsement victories against perceived political foes within the Republican Party. He celebrated after Rep. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.) and Sen. Bill Cassidy (R-La.) lost their primary races to Trump-backed challengers. 

Massie also co-led the congressional effort to release the Epstein files, and critics have suggested his primary loss is connected to his advocacy on this issue.

The Kentucky Republican warned after his loss that Trump’s tactics could imperil the Republican Party’s hold on Congress in the November elections. 

“I think it was dangerous to pare the tree tonight, but that’s what happened,” Massie told MS NOW after his concession speech. “They got pruned, and we’ll see if there’s enough tree left there to win in November and to have a governing majority.”

data.title: Major airline is cutting free in-flight meals, moving to pre-paid menu
data.description:
(KTLA) — Free meals on board Hawaiian Airlines flights will soon be a thing of the past. The airline has announced it will end complimentary service in main cabins in favor of a prepaid option.

Passengers will instead be able to preorder food from a Hawaii-inspired menu curated by Maui-based chef Sheldon Simeon. The program will launch July 1 with a curated set of offerings, with plans to expand the menu in the fall.

According to an announcement by the airline on Thursday, Simeon’s menu will offer “elevated takes on local favorites and comfort foods.”

“By moving to a pre-order model, we’re expanding beyond a single standard meal to offer a broader menu that reflects how our guests want to dine today,” said Hawaiian Airlines Managing Director of Marketing Alisa Onishi.

Other buy-on-board products will remain the same, Onishi said, and the airline will continue to offer free snacks on board.

Prices for each meal range from $11 to $17.

According to the announcement, the menu features dishes such as crispy mochiko chicken with garlic noodles, barbecue teriyaki chicken bento, and corned beef hash with eggs.

New dishes will be announced throughout the year, and items will be rotated.

The meals will be prepared with fresh ingredients no more than 12 hours before each flight, according to the announcement.

Passengers will be able to order through the Alaska-Hawaiian mobile app or website two weeks in advance and up to 20 hours before departure.

Onishi said the airline started work on the change before its integration with Alaska Airlines and had always had Simeon in mind for the project.

data.title: Talarico leads Paxton by 3 points in Texas governor's race post-runoff poll
data.description:
The Democratic candidate in the Texas Senate race has a slight edge over his Republican opponent in a new poll released on Friday.  

Forty-seven percent of likely general election voters in the state backed state Rep. James Talarico (D) in the poll from Texas Public Opinion Research, compared to 44 percent for Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton (R). 

The survey was conducted after the Republican primary runoff contest on Tuesday, in which incumbent Sen. John Cornyn lost to Paxton, who had been endorsed by President Trump.

The latest poll follows a decision from the Cook Political Report, a nonpartisan election handicapper, to adjust its rating of this race from “likely Republican” to “lean Republican” following Paxton’s win. 

Nearly a third of the respondents who voted for Cornyn in the runoff indicated in the poll that they will support the Democratic nominee in the November election. A plurality — 44 percent — of these voters said they will now choose Paxton, and 23 percent were undecided. 

In a social media post following Cornyn’s defeat, Talarico thanked the incumbent senator for his years of public service and extended an olive branch to his supporters.

“We don’t agree on everything, but we both still believe in public service. To Senator Cornyn’s supporters: you have a place in our campaign,” the state representative wrote in the post. 

Of the Cornyn voters who said they will now back Talarico, over half cited the criminal charges against Paxton and corruption concerns. 

Paxton was indicted in 2015 on felony charges in a securities fraud case. Republican state legislators voted to impeach the attorney general in 2023, and he was later acquitted by lawmakers in the Texas state Senate. 

The two candidates have ramped up political advertisements targeting one another’s character and campaigns in the aftermath of the GOP runoff. 

Talarico’s campaign posted Paxton’s mugshot on social media following the state attorney general’s victory and highlighted criticism from GOP state lawmakers from the impeachment vote in another post. 

“Ken Paxton has a criminal record; I have a legislative record,” the state legislator told MS NOW. 

Paxton has criticized the state lawmaker for his previous decision to follow a vegan diet and comment that “God is nonbinary.”

Trump weighed in on the fight in a post on Truth Social following Paxton’s victory, calling Talarico “the worst TEXAS candidate I have ever seen.” 

“A Strong Open Borders advocate, he is WEAK ON CRIME, believes there are 6 genders, is insulting to Jesus Christ, will never support the Military, was a big Mask Wearer until recently, and is a Vegan who dislikes meat, not exactly a good way to be if you’re wanting to win an Election in Texas,” the president wrote in the post. 

Trump also pledged in the post to “do some nice, big, beautiful rallies for Ken.”

The Texas Public Opinion Research poll was conducted from May 27-28 among 1,670 likely Texas voters with a margin of error of approximately 2.8 percentage points.
